name: Wrike
description: Wrike is a work management software used for performance appraisals with custom reports, time tracking, workload overviews, and more. Learn more about Wrike

reviews.review_description: Overall I think Wrike is a very powerful and useful program that would appeal to many businesses and project managers. I've never experienced bugs or glitches like similar apps and is my preferred choice despite the initial difficulty in learning how to properly use and navigate.
reviews.review_pros: Wrike allows you to fully customize it's dashboard in ways other products and apps do not. It makes project tracking and progress monitoring very easy and is visually satisfying.
reviews.review_cons: The learning curve for Wrike is pretty steep and it took considerable time to get my bearings and get comfortable while using the platform.

reviews.review_title: Bleeding-Edge Project Management with Accountability & Transparency
reviews.review_description: Once my team members and I realized that our best approach was to define a process for our company and use Wrike to support that process, Wrike increased our productivity, accuracy, accountability, and transparency.
reviews.review_pros: Wrike offers a robust set of tools that allow me to implement and maintain processes for my organization. The platform is constantly evolving and trying to address my needs as a customer. Wrike has been vital to ensuring no project falls through the cracks and ensuring team accountability.
reviews.review_cons: Sometimes the mandatory changes/removal of legacy features will inconvenience the team. Their constant innovation is a double-edged sword when trying to keep employees up-to-speed with changing practices.

reviews.author_duration: Used the software for: Less than 6 months
reviews.review_description: We have been using Wrike for our real estate agent business and so far the experience has been very mixed at the moment.
reviews.review_pros: What I like about Wrike is the simplified navigation of findings things. It’s easy to get a project up and going in under 30 seconds.
reviews.review_cons: One of the things that I don’t like about, Reich is the interface. It doesn’t have enough features on the free version to compete with platform such as Asana

reviews.review_title: Director of Systems On-site Activities review
reviews.review_description: As the manager who orginally sought a software to implement for a team of persons >40 that allows one place to have a single source of truth related to our projects, the experience has been very good and appears to have long lasting positve impact. The system is good for onoboarding new hires and easy to share information on projects current status and projected status.
reviews.review_pros: Easy to use, configure Spaces to meet the needs of my team and organization. I particullarly like the report creation features that helps us with KPI and provides visibility of our current status to the team. I also like the help boards and the ability to add or vote on new features as needed. Team collaboration on projects has improved and as an operating environment we can ensure all project related information and tasks are within the project, keep focus and not having to dig through emails to find what I am looking for or when.
reviews.review_cons: Licensing costs, seem high when compared with some other solutions, making adding licensing a somewhat complex ROI justification. We have struggled with some features we would like like time keep integration with our ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) software integration (Using SAP), so we are not using Wrike to get a good estimate of estimated verses actual from Wrike, but rather using 2 systems to get to utilization a variance to project estimated hours. I have noticed that some implemented changes by Wrike seem to overwrite my custom settings and changes how the Space or particular screen and tree view are shown, though I did not make a change myself.

reviews.review_title: Why I like Wrike, by Kevin F.
reviews.review_description: Love it and have been using it for a very long time. I did a lot of research many years ago and landed on Wrike -- many years later we were acquired by Allstate, who eventually selected Wrike for the enterprise PM needs -- which was super validating to me. I put my email as kevin.fuschich@ngic.com, which is my login email -- but my true work email is kevin.fuschich@allstate.com
reviews.review_pros: What DID I like? I still use it. But... I think what I like the most is the vastness of my ability to input and track. I am spinning so many plates from so many different universes it's impossible to keep track.
reviews.review_cons: I think it's that I have never been able to figure out how to use milestones... they sound like something super beneficial to me, but I can't figure out how they would be

reviews.review_title: Higher Education Project Management System of Value and Customization
reviews.review_description: My overall experience with Wrike has been mainly positive! I love the customization and ability for teams to work collaboratively across different spaces. We have had a lot of turnover with our account reps and customer success people that makes it difficult at times to find answers. When you have the same group of people working with you and understand how your space is used it's a lot easier on the customer to want to continue to use the product and find the support we need.
reviews.review_pros: I like the customization opportunities the most. I am able to have multiple teams with different needs under one roof but they are all able to make specific requests for their space and Wrike is able to accommodate all these needs. Tracking tasks is also equally helpful and I have found the automation features help streamline all the different tasks we have at once.
reviews.review_cons: My least favorite thing about Wrike would be the lack of function mobile apps/views. I find that there are lots of bugs and it doesn't work the same way it does on desktop. For a team on the go a lot it is helpful to have a system that can accommodate mobile view.

reviews.review_title: Smart way to track Teams productivity
reviews.review_description: I use Wrike daily to keep myself on track without having to meet with our team regularly. Dependencies help notify me when I'm up to work on a project.
reviews.review_pros: Tracking of my daily to dos with visibility for the whole team. Workload charts to easily drag and drop tasks to fit my schedule. The ability to build the system out to work for our needs. Customer support was very responsive to any questions and troubleshooting. A great way manage our work throughout the entire organization and maintain visibility. Easy way to track our teams productivity!
reviews.review_cons: We had to build the system out to work for our needs which causes some inconsistencies on how we used the system across business units.

reviews.review_title: The First List I Check Each Day
reviews.review_description: It's the one source of truth I can always depend on to help me get everything done quickly and efficiently. Everyone who works with me knows, "If it's not in Wrike, it doesn't exist!" :)
reviews.review_pros: I have been a long-time user of Wrike and find it especially useful for planning and tracking my numerous copywriting projects. I created my own personal dashboard, and I check it every day to stay one step ahead of my always-evolving workflow.
reviews.review_cons: I really would love to sort my customized "starred task" widget by date, so the projects that are due first are at the top. (This functionality is currently not available.)

reviews.review_description: Overall, we have had a positive experience with Wrike over the past ~7 years. As our processes have evolved, we've been able to flex our Wrike instance to fit the basic needs of project intake, tracking, and capacity monitoring. We can easily assign and reassign resources to various initiatives. Reporting is decent - there is ability to create different views for leadership vs. individuals who need more detail.
reviews.review_pros: Wrike is a great out-of-the-box tool for workflow management. We use it to manage research pipelines, and it is easy to intake new projects and assign resources to work on those projects. We can then track individual projects, manage resource capacity, and have a view into the stages of the overall research process, all without having to code anything custom.
reviews.review_cons: I wish integration with other tools was more seamless, and updating the existing tickets was easier for stakeholder groups who are not using wrike as a part of their main workflow.

reviews.review_description: I’ve really enjoyed Wrike and our company uses it daily to keep track of strategic initiatives, department initiatives and I even use it to track some personal things!!
reviews.review_pros: The ability to have multiple projects in one space. The workflow management is key. I think it’s a very user friendly platform- once you become aware of all the capabilities. We like the use of the request forms for many reasons. DADHBOARDS! Wow! If I haven’t made a dashboard - you need to! It keeps teams aligned and up to date on current tasks!
reviews.review_cons: I wish there was a way to show pictures on the request forms. Users filling out the request form can add pics - but it would be nice to add as an admin when setting up the forms.

reviews.review_title: A Reliable Workflow Engine for a Small but Fast-Moving Team
reviews.author_duration: Used the software for: Less than 6 months
reviews.review_description: As CEO of a small, fast-growing company, Wrike has become the backbone of our operations. It centralized our work, improved communication, and helped us scale our processes without adding headcount. While support could be stronger, the platform consistently delivers real business value, making it a reliable long-term solution for us.
reviews.review_pros: Wrike gives my 5-person team the structure and automation we need to work like a larger organization. The workflow customization, dashboards, and task tracking reduce friction and make client delivery more predictable. Integrations with Slack and Gmail simplify communication, and the upcoming additions of Google Drive and GitHub will streamline things even further.
reviews.review_cons: Customer support is not the fastest, and responses can feel generic, but it hasn’t been a deal-breaker. There are also occasional UI quirks and small learning curves for new team members, though the benefits outweigh those bumps.

reviews.review_description: Overall experience has been very good. The platform is user-friendly (most of the time) and it's allowed me to set processes, workflows and automations. It's great for project and campaign management and I'd recommend to any company looking for a platform that Wrike is the one to go with.
reviews.review_pros: It's user-friendly, robust, aesthetically pleasing and I love creating blueprints and automations and seeing them work properly!
reviews.review_cons: There's no sort of filter option on the automations (besides date and owner), making it difficult to find a specific one - no name filter and I have to open the automation to see the entire title. It's a burden and very time-consuming when I have hundreds of automations in place.

reviews.review_description: Much better than our old system of physical folders. Makes it easy to transition from in office work to remote.
reviews.review_pros: The best thing about Wrike is the ability to review artwork from graphic designers. Instead of printing and re-printing over and over, we can just look at the most recent version.
reviews.review_cons: Our approval structure is set up so that 1 of 3 people can approve a project to the next stage, but Wrike is unable to mirror this process.

reviews.review_description: I used Wrike at my previous company, loved it, and actually onboarded my team onto it when I moved jobs to another org. I appreciate that they have a user advocacy platform that gamifies their participation and encourages helping others, learning, and you can even earn swag. It's a fun thing I try to do every day when I have a few min, and it's always a nice break from the monotony of work.
reviews.review_pros: I love the flexibility of the tool. At first, it's very intimidating because of how robust it is, but once you dive in and understand the capabilities and how to use them to your advantage, it's great. There's not one way to use the system, you can very much shape it into what you need that is significantly different than how someone else might use it. It molds to your needs, you don't have to mold your needs to its capabilities.
reviews.review_cons: Some of the tools feel a little underdeveloped, like Calendars and reports. Don't get me wrong, they have great capabilities and filters, but the potential behind these tools could be exponentially better.

reviews.review_description: Good. Support is great overall, slow response times for European timezones. Very high AI-Feature push currently. Missing core updates to the regular product.
reviews.review_pros: Clean and slick design. Open to all platforms (iOS, Android, MacOS, Windows,..). Easy access ( no need to configure any file server sharing)
reviews.review_cons: Perfomance. Perfomance has been degrading over time. Many Functions loved by customers have been discontinued ( List view)

reviews.review_title: Engaged with the Future, Hopeful Wrike can Scale to meet it
reviews.review_description: I work in marketing operations. I operationalize all of the work that we do to serve our customers. We work as a marketing agency of sorts for doctor's offices. We are in the process of a re-org which is expanding our use of task tracking and Wrike Spaces, something we haven't heavily utilized in the past. I am excited to see what Wrike can do for us in those realms.
reviews.review_pros: I like that I can create a workspace unique to my needs. It is an excellent product that allows for unique customizations for any type of work. I am excited to see what AI can do for Wrike as well. AI will be able to take the building blocks of Wrike and really put it into overdrive which will be exciting. But I am a little concerned whether or not Wrike can scale to meet the customer demands. Right now Wrike has become a SLOW product (as in, page load times) and our experience with Customer Success over the last few years has left us feeling like our use case is not well understood.
reviews.review_cons: Frankly, we have struggled to have a Customer Success Manager that serves our needs aptly for quite some time. We are looking for someone we can partner with to help us when we as admins have failed to figure out a solution to our needs within the product. Typically, our Customer Success Managers will take it back to their product team, and we won't hear anything about it. Or get told it is not possible. It would be nice if our Customer Success Manager felt like a partner that helped us solve complex problems but it seems like the knowledge of our use case with the product just isn't there. This is unfortunate as we are long time Wrike user and it seems like our Customer Success Manager's struggle to answer questions about our use case. It would be nice if that experience was more tailored to how we use Wrike, versus a blanket Wrike customer experience. Also, this is timing, but as an Enterprise customer, we have really struggled to track how often tasks are being completed after the due date. The fact that you list Task Tracking as a considered topic, but figuring out a way to track that information in an Enterprise account is not at all user-friendly. Perhaps it would be easier if we used the time-tracking feature, however, that is a level of oversight we would prefer not to utilize. And manually updating the amount of time overdue that task was, defeats a purpose that Wrike should absolutely be able to serve. ALSO, and this is a BIG one. Wrike has been SO SLOW, ever since you got rid of list view and introduced Table View. As an admin, on a daily basis, I get complaints from users that Wrike is slow to load. Dashboards take lightyears to load, specifically with the more filters you put into a widget. I feel like I when I use filters I am using Wrike to its fullest capabilities, but am then penalized because of the amount of time things take to load. This literally costs the organization time waiting for Wrike to load. Hugely, hugely unfortunate.

reviews.review_description: Overall, Wrike is a strong project management solution for teams handling complex, multi‑step work who need visibility and accountability. It’s not a plug‑and‑play tool, but once workflows, fields, and dashboards are set up correctly, it becomes a reliable central hub for managing tasks, timelines, and collaboration. While there is an initial learning curve, the payoff is better organization, fewer follow‑ups, and clearer ownership across projects. I truly couldn't imagine going thru a workday without it!
reviews.review_pros: Wrike is a great tool if you’re juggling a lot at once and need everything to live in one place. It’s especially strong for teams running complex projects with lots of moving parts. For example, you can build custom workflows for different types of work; creative approvals, production timelines, operational request - so a design task doesn’t move the same way a finance or logistics task does. Dashboards and timelines make it easy to see what’s on track, what’s slipping, and who’s overloaded, which is helpful when you’re managing multiple teams or long-term programs. Wrike’s integrations are also a big win; being able to connect it to tools like Outlook, Teams, Box keep communication and files tied directly to the work.
reviews.review_cons: Wrike can feel like a lot at first. We joke that it feels like drinking out a fire hose (mostly due to how much we rely of it as a business). If you’re coming from a simpler tool, the number of features - folders, spaces, custom fields, automations, can be overwhelming without some time invested in learning. For example, if everyone creates tasks their own way or names things differently, things can get messy fast. (Same goes for Custom Fields - lock those down!) I do think that with custom service for a ticket, it's sometimes hard to get bounced from rep to rep depending on times zones.

reviews.review_description: My overall experience with Wrike was positive, especially for managing complex, cross‑functional work. It’s a very capable platform once it’s set up well. The ability to customize workflows, views, and automation made it much easier to align teams around shared processes while still supporting different working styles. Collaboration improved because conversations, files, and approvals lived alongside the work instead of being scattered across email and chat. The trade‑off is that you do need clear ownership and governance; without that, things can become messy or confusing over time. With strong admin practices, though, Wrike is a powerful backbone for project and portfolio management.
reviews.review_pros: What I liked most about Wrike was how it combined flexibility with structure. The platform makes it easy to manage complex, cross‑functional projects while still giving teams freedom to work in the way that suits them best. Customizable workflows, dashboards, and automation help keep everyone aligned without adding unnecessary bureaucracy. Real‑time visibility into tasks, dependencies, and workloads improves accountability and makes it much easier to spot issues early. I also appreciated the strong collaboration features—comments, proofing, and approvals all in one place—which significantly reduced back‑and‑forth emails and helped decisions move faster.
reviews.review_cons: What I liked least about Wrike was the learning curve and occasional complexity as usage scaled. For new or non‑technical users, the sheer number of features, views, and configuration options can feel overwhelming and require meaningful onboarding time. As workspaces grow, it’s easy for structures, custom fields, and workflows to become inconsistent if there isn’t strong admin governance and clear standards. The interface, while powerful, can feel cluttered when many projects and automations are in play, and performance can sometimes lag with very large data sets. Reporting is solid but can require effort to tailor exactly to leadership’s needs.

reviews.review_description: I'm considered a curious mind and a lover of process, so my experience has been great, because I am curious about how all the menus and buttons in general work or what they offer to then see how we can use Wrike better. Wrike certainly helps as being the source of truth for my projects. When a meeting occurs about something in Wrike, I pull it up and add any notes right into it. No splitting between an email inbox, Google Drive notes (although we link those all the time for briefs), and Slack messages. I like that I can dump my thoughts into projects and set up subtasks just how I like and then not have to think about that thing until it is ready to be worked on, on the list of priorities. My dashboard helps me not loose sight of it and keeps my productivity up.
reviews.review_pros: I like that I can build dashboards to fit the needs of what I personally want to see in order to manage what is assigned to me (for example: A widget for work tagged as analytics to gather, a widget for work due in the next week, a widget for work that helps our business on Amazon...etc) and dashboards for a specific department to fit their needs. I open Wrike daily and it's so nice to see what is "Ready", as our workflow status, for me in my dashboard as well. We also use the file commenting feature very frequently, that really cuts down on meetings and understanding the vision between team members. I also like that you can submit ideas for the tool at any time.
reviews.review_cons: I think the the inbox structure could use improvements. Could add a search bar for the inbox that lets you search for date ranges of a comment, keywords someone might have commented with as another way to find things (for example: I might remember that so-and-so tagged me with the word 'retailers' in their comment, but I don't remember which subject that was for and I had already had the inbox message 'read'.) Something more similar to an email inbox. I also think if you are using the "approvals" process on assets, that it would be nice to see in my 'sent' inbox that the approval I am requesting shows up there. Some times when I use that feature I need to then track down the ticket again the same day and it could be easier that way.

reviews.author_profession: Director of Web and Organic Search
reviews.review_description: Overall, my experience with Wrike is fantastic. It allows people from multiple different departments at my organization to collaborate in a very simple and easy way for example, compliance uses it for contract. Review the marketing team we made our own form for accepting external request from other teams and the customization of the forms make it really easy to get exactly the information we need to do our work efficiently and in a timely manner, but overall, yeah, I love Rex flexibility, and customization possibilities
reviews.review_pros: I love the integrations and the flexibility of how you can present the different task items for example for one board we use just a standard table view, but for my testing board, I use a column board view so I can actually show the tasks moving through the process as they progress and I really like having flexibility so that you could present the work the best way it can be.
reviews.review_cons: The thing I like the least about Wrike is probably the email immigration functionality where I do love the fact that you can respond to a comment but I wish I could do an emoji response or actually tag someone in the response so that I don't have to go into the app directly itself if I manage most of my communication through email

reviews.review_description: t genuinely helps keep projects aligned, teams informed, and deadlines predictable, which makes day-to-day work feel more grounded and less reactive. At the same time, there are moments when the system’s complexity peeks through and adds a little extra effort to otherwise simple tasks. Still, the benefits outweigh the bumps. When everything is flowing, Wrike becomes a reliable backbone for managing creative chaos and keeping the entire process moving with purpose and clarity.
reviews.review_pros: What I appreciate most about Wrike is how it brings clarity to the chaos without getting in the way. It keeps projects organized, communication tight, and deliverables on track, all while feeling surprisingly intuitive. The ability to see workloads, timelines, and approvals in one place makes collaboration smoother and eliminates that constant, nagging uncertainty about who’s doing what. It’s the rare tool that genuinely lightens the load instead of adding to it.
reviews.review_cons: It can feel a bit heavy at times, like it’s trying to do everything all at once. Some features hide behind too many clicks, and small tasks can end up feeling more complicated than they should be. When I’m moving fast, the interface occasionally slows me down instead of helping me get out of my own way. It’s great when it’s dialed in, but it definitely has moments where it feels more like managing the tool than managing the work.

reviews.review_pros: Wrike makes our creative workflow so much more seamless. It's great to have a one-stop-shop for our creative team to post, review, and complete work. Using it as our source of truth has made us much more efficient.
reviews.review_cons: I am incredibly happy with Wrike and don't have any major bugs/issues in mind. I can't fathom going back to not using a system as intuitive and helpful as Wrike.

reviews.review_title: Wrike is great for collaboration openness
reviews.review_description: It has improved the overall completion of our time critical tasks, works well with collaboration and has no reliability issues.
reviews.review_pros: I have personally used it to delegate tasks and it’s easy to share project updates with my manager to make sure our field projects are documented accurately.
reviews.review_cons: The setup of complex workflows requires a bit of tinkering so sometimes we have to call our technician to ask how to do something.

reviews.review_title: Wrike – you simply can’t do without it
reviews.review_description: Generally speaking, I was sceptical when, about a year and a half ago, we were introduced to Wrike to manage our various digital campaigns alongside our offshore teams; I thought it was just another thing to do. However, over time, Wrike has proved to be essential for saving time, keeping things organised and making communication easier. It has replaced many tools, and we now have at least 50% of our workflow consolidated into a single platform.
reviews.review_pros: When it comes to managing workflows and organising various tasks, it’s something my team can no longer do without. What I like most is definitely the ability to see, step by step and in real time, what a colleague is doing on a particular task, including the time they expect to spend on it.
reviews.review_cons: Wrike has so many features that it can seem overwhelming at first. The guides aren’t particularly clear if you’ve never used a platform like this before, so you need to set aside time to learn the various features. If you don’t have the time, it can be difficult to get to grips with, but if you do have the time to familiarise yourself with how Wrike works, it becomes indispensable for your day-to-day work.

reviews.review_title: Strong for Complex Teams, Heavy for Simple Workflows
reviews.review_description: Overall, Wrike has been a powerful tool for structured project and process management. Once configured, it becomes a reliable hub for collaboration, approvals, and tracking work across teams. It’s not the most beginner‑friendly platform, but for teams that need depth, automation, and strong workflow control, it delivers solid results.
reviews.review_pros: Wrike’s workflow customization is its strongest asset. Being able to build tailored workflows, automate repetitive steps, and switch between multiple views (Gantt, board, table, calendar) makes it great for managing complex, multi‑step projects. The visibility across teams is also a big plus once everything is set up.
reviews.review_cons: The learning curve is steep. The interface can feel cluttered, and many of the most useful features sit behind higher‑tier plans. Performance can lag on large workspaces, and occasional UI quirks—like slow loading or inconsistent notifications—can interrupt momentum.
